title: Cat Eating Plastic Bags: Risks, Causes, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat eating plastic bags? Learn about pica, the risks of intestinal blockage, and when you need to contact an emergency veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ingesting plastic can lead to dangerous intestinal blockages in cats; monitor for vomiting or lethargy and seek veterinary care immediately if your cat shows signs of distress.

What does it mean if my cat is chewing on or eating plastic bags?

When a cat eats or chews on non-food items like plastic bags, it is a behavior known as pica. Cats may be drawn to plastic because of the crinkly sound, the scent of food residue, or even the chemicals used in the manufacturing process, such as cornstarch or tallow. While it may seem like a harmless quirk, ingesting plastic can lead to serious health complications.

Is it a veterinary emergency if my cat swallowed plastic?

  • Urgency Level: Medium.
  • Primary Risks: Swallowing large pieces or long strips poses a significant risk of gastrointestinal obstruction or choking.
  • When to call a vet: Monitor your cat closely for distress. If you notice vomiting, a sudden loss of appetite, lethargy, or a lack of bowel movements, contact a veterinarian immediately.

Why should I take a photo of the chewed plastic for the vet?

  • Identify Materials: A clear image helps the medical team identify exactly what kind of material was consumed.
  • Estimate Size: Seeing the chewed area allows the vet to estimate the size of the missing piece.
  • Determine Treatment: Knowing if the plastic is thin and soft or thick and sharp helps the vet decide between monitoring for natural passing, inducing vomiting, or surgical intervention.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Cats that ingest non-nutritive substances such as plastic bags are at risk for gastrointestinal obstruction. Foreign bodies such as plastic, which cannot be digested, may cause partial or complete obstruction. Linear foreign bodies, common in cats, can cause significant GI mucosal damage and devitalization, potentially affecting a large section of the GI tract and requiring resection and anastomosis. Radiographs can be used to monitor the passage of small objects, but failure to pass within 48 hours may warrant surgical intervention.

Chapter: Behavioral Medicine, Dermatology, Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1574)

faqs.question: Why is my cat suddenly obsessed with plastic?
faqs.answer: Sudden interest in plastic can be caused by boredom, dental pain, or nutritional deficiencies. It is always best to mention this behavior to your vet during a check-up.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat something to help the plastic pass?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting or give your cat laxatives without professional medical advice, as this can sometimes make the situation worse depending on the type of plastic.

faqs.question: How long does it take for a cat to pass a foreign object?
faqs.answer: If an object is small enough to pass, it typically takes between 10 to 24 hours to move through the entire digestive tract.
